Usage:
  USE USAGE              (Show this usage)
  USE <table>            (Open <DBF slot>/<table>.dbf in current area)
  USE <table.dbf>        (Open named DBF; logical names resolve through DBF slot)
  USE <path\\table.dbf>   (Open explicit path)
  USE <table> NOINDEX    (Open in physical order; skip index auto-attach)
  USE <table> NOIDX      (Alias of NOINDEX)
Notes:
  - USE closes/resets the current area before opening the target table.
  - USE prevents duplicate opens of the same DBF path across work areas.
  - USE auto-attaches memo storage when memo fields are present.
  - USE auto-attaches flavor-appropriate indexes when present, unless NOINDEX/NOIDX is used.
  - USE prefers the configured INDEXES slot and falls back to the DBF directory.
  - x64/v128 tables prefer CDX.
  - x32 tables prefer CNX, then INX.

MESSAGING BLACKBOX
  DATA IN: hard-coded text, message IDs, message arguments, locale/language rows
  PROCESS: extract, catalog, localize, validate placeholders, replace source strings gradually
  OUT: x64base message catalog, localized runtime text, typed warnings/errors/status/help messages
  CONTROL: SET LANGUAGE / SET LOCALE selects message-rendering locale where supported.

MAINT BOUNDARY
  first-wave MAINT is inspection-only.
  It must not mutate:
    - source files
    - HELP DATA or raw HELP DBFs
    - CMDHELPCHK expectations
    - metadata catalogs
    - DBF/CDX/LMDB artifacts
    - runtime scripts
    - publications or media
  Mutation lanes require separate guarded packages and explicit authorization.

Usage:
  CALC USAGE             (Show this usage)
  CALC <expr>            (Evaluate expression and print result)
  CALC (<expr>)          (Outer parentheses are allowed)
  CALC <field> = <expr>  (If <field> exists, delegate to CALCWRITE)
Examples:
  CALC 1 + 2
  CALC DATE()
  CALC UPPER(LNAME)
  CALC BALANCE = BALANCE + 10
Notes:
  - CALC expression-only mode is read-only.
  - CALC field-assignment mode mutates through CALCWRITE.
  - Empty CALC preserves existing behavior and prints .F.

Usage:
  CALCWRITE USAGE
  CALCWRITE <field> = <expr>
Examples:
  CALCWRITE BALANCE = BALANCE + 10
  CALCWRITE LNAME = UPPER(LNAME)
  CALCWRITE POSTED = TODAY
Notes:
  - CALCWRITE requires an open table and a current record.
  - RHS expressions are evaluated with xexpr against the current area.
  - Values are normalized and validated for the target field type.
  - X64 memo fields update memo payloads and store memo object-id text.
  - TABLE ON buffers changes and marks fields stale/dirty.
  - TABLE OFF writes through DbArea::replaceFieldStored for index-safe mutation.

Usage:

  REPLACE 3 WITH TODAY
Notes:
  - REPLACE requires an open table and a current record.
  - RHS values are evaluated before validation/storage.
  - X64 memo text is converted to stored memo object-id text.
  - TABLE ON buffers field changes and marks fields stale/dirty.
  - TABLE OFF writes immediately through DbArea storage.

Notes:

  - COPY TO <name>                 : binary copy of the current DBF
  - COPY TO <name> AS <flavor>     : logical table copy/conversion
  - COPY TO <name> AS X64 VECTOR   : one-step copy from any open table to x64 vector form
  - VECTOR is target-driven and is valid only with AS X64

  - AS VFP/FOX/MSDOS writes free-table 10-byte descriptor field names

ABOUT - Page 1 of 2
===================

DotTalk++

Author
 Derald Grimwood

Dedicated to
 Kathy Grimwood

Project
 DotTalk++ is a modern C++ xBase-inspired database runtime and command shell.

Heritage
 The DotTalk++ command model draws inspiration from the classic xBase
 lineage of database languages:

 dBase - early interactive database shell
 Clipper - compiled xBase systems
 FoxPro - relational navigation and index-driven querying

 DotTalk++ preserves many of the familiar commands and workflows from
 these systems while extending them with:

 modern help catalogs
 relational traversal (REL ENUM)
 tuple projection
 scripting and automation

 DotTalk++ also combines aspects of both the interactive xBase model
 and the compiled application model. Like dBase and FoxPro, it provides
 a live command shell for exploring data. Like Clipper, it can be extended
 through source code and compiled. It is also structured as modular
 runtime libraries, including xbase and xindex, beneath the shell.

History
 The project traces back to 1993, when Derald Grimwood wrote a small
 ANSI C database program as a practical and experimental system,
 including fixed-length record storage and a simple in-memory B-tree.

 In 2025, that earlier work was revived and used as the conceptual basis
 for a modern 64-bit rebuild in C++. The result became DotTalk++:
 not a direct port, but a broader reimplementation and expansion.

Current Direction
 DotTalk++ aims to retain the clarity of the xBase interaction model
 while making the engine suitable for modern experimentation and
 education.

 It is intended to serve as:
 - a working DBF database runtime
 - a relational exploration environment
 - a scripting and automation shell
 - a teaching system for database concepts

 Internally, DotTalk++ is also organized as a modular system:
 - xbase : core DBF/table/runtime library
 - xindex : indexing library
 - dottalk : command shell and interactive environment

 In this sense, DotTalk++ sits between FoxPro and Clipper:
 - interactive and stateful like FoxPro
 - extensible and compilable like Clipper

Design Philosophy
 DotTalk++ is intentionally stateful and interactive.

 It exposes important runtime concepts directly, including:
 - current work area
 - current record pointer
 - active order/index
 - active filter
 - relation graph
 - buffering state

 The goal is to make database behavior visible and understandable during
 live operation, rather than hiding it behind abstraction.

Working Model
 DotTalk++ can be understood as four cooperating layers:

 1. Command Layer - interactive commands and scripting
 2. Data Layer - tables, records, fields, indexes
 3. Logic Layer - expressions, predicates, control flow
 4. Projection Layer - LIST, SMARTLIST, TUPLE, REL ENUM, browsers

 Compiler    : {compiler}

Years
 Origin: 1993 ANSI C
 Revival / C++ X64 modern rebuild: 2025-

Summary
 DotTalk++ honors the xBase tradition while extending it into a modern,
 teachable, experiment-friendly database runtime.

 User interfaces change, languages change, but the underlying database principles remain constant. -- Derald Grimwood

Notes:
  - BBOX is read-only and educational.
  - BBOX teaches the Blackbox model: data in, processing, information out.
  - BBOX does not mutate DBFs, HELP, META, CMDHELPCHK, source, scripts, or publications.

BLACKBOX MODEL
  DATA IN
    source comments, usage contracts, DBFs, scripts, Markdown, media, source code, catalogs

  PROCESSING
    scan, harvest, classify, import, build, validate, publish, smoke test

  INFORMATION OUT
    HELP, CMDHELP, manuals, data dictionary, comments workspace, MAN*/DD*/SRC* catalogs, reports, diagrams

  CONTROL
    backup, rollback, boundary ledger, runtime smoke, savepoint, next gate

BBOX LANES
  COMMENTS   source comments and @dottalk.usage -> SRC* evidence catalogs
  HELP       registry, DOTREF, usage contracts -> HELP DATA and CMDHELP
  MANUALGEN  manual sections/media/manifests -> MAN* catalog and published manuals
  DATADICT   repo/schema/help evidence -> DD* catalog and DDICT runtime view
  MESSAGING  hard-coded text/message IDs/locales -> typed localized runtime messages
  CMDHELPCHK command/help contracts -> validation evidence
  MAINT      maintenance lanes, cookbooks, gates, and read-only status

COMMENTS BLACKBOX
  DATA IN: source files, header comments, @dottalk.usage v1 blocks
  PROCESS: harvest, classify, import, validate, review disposition
  OUT: SRCFILE, SRCBLOCK, SRCLINE, SRCUSAGE, SRCCLASS, SRCDISP, SRCALIAS, MEMO_LINES
  CURRENT WORKSPACE: dottalkpp/data/workspaces/comments.dtschema

HELP BLACKBOX
  DATA IN: command registry, dotref.hpp, foxref.hpp, usage contracts, curated rows, source-mined facts
  PROCESS: CMDHELP BUILD, HELP DATA generation, validation, runtime smoke
  OUT: help_line.dbf, help_topic.dbf, help_artifacts.dbf, commands.dbf, cmd_args.dbf, HELP/CMDHELP output
  NOTE: CMDHELP and HELP /DOT are related consumers but not identical proof surfaces.

MANUALGEN BLACKBOX
  DATA IN: section files, appendices, media anchors, review queues, manifests
  PROCESS: assemble, normalize, validate, publish, catalog, runtime smoke
  OUT: published manual, MAN* catalog, MANUAL runtime command, regeneration evidence

DATADICT BLACKBOX
  DATA IN: data dictionary manifests, DD* candidate rows, x64 DATA_DICTIONARY_* physical tables
  PROCESS: stage, import, validate, CDX/LMDB build, runtime smoke
  OUT: DD* catalog, DDICT STATUS/TABLES/OBJECTS/FIELDS/TAGS/REL/EVIDENCE
  RULE: use bridge policy; report metadata owner and physical artifact.

MAINT BLACKBOX
  PURPOSE: developer/SDLC maintenance inspection over documented lanes and cookbooks
  PRIMARY APP: native C++ MAINT surface, planned separately
  EXTERNAL TOOLS: Python 3.12 for portable report tooling; PowerShell only for temporary scaffolding
  RULE: MAINT starts read-only; mutation lanes require explicit guarded packages.

Usage:

Notes:
  - CLOSE closes the current work area.
  - CLOSE ALL clears all relations before closing the current work area.

Usage:
  CDX USAGE
  CDX INFO [<path.cdx>]
  CDX TAGS [<path.cdx>]
  CDX CREATE [<path.cdx>]
  CDX ADDTAG <name> [<path.cdx>]
  CDX DROPTAG <name> [<path.cdx>]
Notes:
  - CDX with no arguments shows usage.
  - CREATE refuses to overwrite an existing CDX file.
  - INFO/TAGS inspect metadata; ADDTAG/DROPTAG mutate tag metadata.

  CNX USAGE

  CNX TAGS [<path.cnx>]

  CNX DROPTAG <name> [<path.cnx>]
  CNX WALK <tag> [<path.cnx>]
  CNX TRACE <tag> [<path.cnx>]
Notes:
  - CNX with no arguments shows usage.
  - CREATE refuses to overwrite an existing CNX file.
  - INFO/TAGS/WALK/TRACE inspect metadata; ADDTAG/DROPTAG mutate tag metadata.
